title: The Monster
description: A politically charged Albanian novel whose allegory became unacceptable under dictatorship.
descriptionParagraphs: A politically charged Albanian novel whose allegory became unacceptable under dictatorship.

The Monster is usually read through its treatment of dictatorship, allegory, and state paranoia. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes dictatorship, allegory, and state paranoia feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A politically charged Albanian novel whose allegory became unacceptable under dictatorship.
banningParagraphs: The Monster entered censorship debates as a novel associated with dictatorship, allegory, and state paranoia. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political dissent and ideological control.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1965-1990 in Albania, where Communist authorities banned circulation. Kadare's novel was suppressed for decades in communist Albania. It is one of the most important Albanian examples in the seed dataset.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
